one_line_takeaway: SolAgent shows that wiring an LLM code-generation agent directly into Forge (compiler/tests) and Slither (static analysis) feedback loops substantially raises Solidity Pass@1 (up to 64.39% vs ~25% vanilla) and cuts Slither-flagged vulnerabilities by up to 39.77% versus human-written code, with Forge/compiler feedback being the single largest driver of correctness gains.
key_finding: On SolEval+ (81 files, 1,188 tests), with Claude-Sonnet-4.5 as backbone SolAgent reaches Pass@1 = 64.39% (765/1188 tests, 95.06% compile rate) vs. 25.59% for vanilla Claude-Sonnet-4.5 and 28.37% for the best agent baseline (Qwen-Agent); with GPT-5-Mini SolAgent gets 56.65% Pass@1 (vs. ~14-21% baselines) and with GPT-5.1 it gets 54.71% (vs. ~10-19% baselines). Security: SolAgent(GPT-5-Mini) reduces Slither-detected vulnerabilities by 39.77% versus the human-written baseline repo code (259->156, common-file basis), SolAgent(Claude) by 15.70% (293->247), SolAgent(GPT-5.1) by 24.79% (242->182). Ablation: removing Forge feedback causes the largest Pass@1 drop (Claude: 64.39%->26.18%; GPT-5-Mini: 56.65%->17.17%; GPT-5.1: 54.71%->18.86%), identifying compiler/test feedback as the dominant driver of correctness; removing Slither feedback has mixed effect on vulnerability count at the standard stopping round but at the Min-Vuln round increases minimum achievable vulnerabilities by 23.76%-34.83% across models, showing Slither feedback expands the reachable secure-solution space; removing file-system tools drops Pass@1 by up to ~23 points (GPT-5.1: 54.71%->31.73%). Distillation: base Qwen3-8B achieves only 5.88% compile rate / 0.33% Pass@1 on trajectories' held-out split; the best distilled variant, SolAgent-tracker-v2, reaches 17.65% compile rate / 1.31% Pass@1, matching Qwen3-32B's 1.31% Pass@1 (35.29% compile rate) despite being 4x smaller, and backward-token-truncation (v2, keeping the final 4K tokens containing the code output) consistently beats forward truncation (v1).
core_contribution: SolAgent, a tool-augmented dual-agent (Coding Agent + Refining Agent) framework for repository-level Solidity smart contract generation. It runs a dual-loop refinement process: an inner loop that calls the Forge compiler/test framework for functional-correctness feedback, and an outer loop that calls the Slither static analyzer for security-vulnerability feedback, plus file-system tools (list/read) so the Refining Agent can resolve project dependencies. A Dynamic Stopping Algorithm halts refinement on success, pass-rate stagnation over N=2 rounds, or feedback-similarity oscillation (SequenceMatcher ratio > tau=0.9), up to a max of 50 rounds. The paper also distills SolAgent's interaction trajectories into a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B model (via full-parameter fine-tuning on ms-swift/8x Ascend 910B2 NPUs) to obtain a cheaper single-pass generator, and constructs a new benchmark, SolEval+, by augmenting the existing SolEval dataset (1,125 functions) with 1,188 Foundry-executable unit tests across 81 files/1,908 methods.
threat_model: No adversarial threat model against SolAgent or its pipeline is defined; this is not an attack/defense paper. The 'security' dimension is defensive/code-quality in nature: the paper treats Slither-detected static-analysis alerts (categorized High/Medium/Low severity, e.g. reentrancy, integer overflow, access-control issues) in LLM-generated Solidity code as the object to be minimized. The one illustrated vulnerability class is reentrancy (Fig. 4, claimThrone example: baseline code sends Ether via king.call{value: msg.value}('') before updating the king/prize state, which SolAgent fixes by reordering to checks-effects-interactions). There is no threat actor, attack surface, or exploit modeling beyond 'the generated contract may contain classes of bugs that a static analyzer can flag.'
limitations: Absolute Pass@1 for the distilled 8B models remains extremely low (<=1.31%), so the 'matches Qwen3-32B' claim is a match at a near-floor performance level, not a strong result in absolute terms. Gas efficiency is not uniformly improved: SolAgent consumes 1.44x-2.39x more gas (mean pairwise ratio) than GPT-5-Mini-based baselines, a trade-off the authors attribute to prioritizing correctness/security over gas optimization. Two SolAgent/backbone combinations (SolAgent-Summary variant with Claude-Sonnet-4.5 and with GPT-5.1) actually show a net *increase* in vulnerability count versus the human-written repo baseline (+26.29% and +25.27% respectively, Table 5), showing the security benefit is not universal across configurations. SolAgent also produces substantially more tokens, LOC, and cyclomatic complexity than baselines (e.g., 216.4 avg LOC vs. 148.7 for vanilla Claude), which the paper frames positively as 'more complete' but is also a cost/maintainability trade-off not deeply analyzed. Security evaluation relies solely on one static analyzer (Slither) as ground truth for 'vulnerabilities' with no dynamic/fuzzing validation (e.g., Echidna) or manual audit, and no comparison against dedicated LLM vulnerability-repair baselines (GPTScan, ContractTinker, SmartInv) that are discussed only in Related Work, not benchmarked. The preliminary study (Table 1) motivating the work is based on just 50 generated contracts.

datasets_used: SolEval+ Benchmark (constructed by the authors, extending SolEval): 81 files, 1,908 methods/declarations, 1,188 Foundry-executable unit tests, derived from 1,125 functions in the SolEval repository-level Solidity dataset

Fine-tuning trajectory datasets collected from SolAgent runs: dataset_tracker (1,132 samples, 9,007 total messages) and dataset_mix (1,985 samples, 15,064 total messages), split 80% train / 20% held-out test
quantitative_results: Pass@1: SolAgent 64.39% (Claude-Sonnet-4.5) vs vanilla LLM 25.59% vs best agent baseline Qwen-Agent 28.37%
Compilation rate: SolAgent 95.06% (Claude) vs vanilla LLM baselines 30%-45%
Vulnerability reduction vs human-written baseline repo: -15.70% (Claude, 293->247), -39.77% (GPT-5-Mini, 259->156), -24.79% (GPT-5.1, 242->182)
Ablation w/o Slither at Min-Vuln round: minimum vulnerabilities increase +23.76% (Claude), +26.25% (GPT-5-Mini), +34.83% (GPT-5.1)
Distillation: base Qwen3-8B 5.88% compile rate / 0.33% Pass@1; SolAgent-tracker-v2 17.65% compile rate / 1.31% Pass@1, matching Qwen3-32B's 1.31% Pass@1 (35.29% compile rate)
Gas efficiency: SolAgent mean pairwise gas ratio ~0.9958-1.0296 vs Claude-based baselines (near parity) but 1.44x-2.39x vs GPT-5-Mini-based baselines
Token cost: SolAgent (Claude) uses 65,510 avg total prompt tokens/file vs MetaGPT's 137,560 prompt tokens/file for a 5.5x Pass@1 improvement (64.39% vs 11.78%)
baselines_compared: Vanilla LLMs: Claude-Sonnet-4.5, GPT-5-Mini, GPT-5.1 (prompted directly)
Agent frameworks: GitHub Copilot, DeepCode, MetaGPT, Qwen-Agent (each run with the same three backbone models)
Human-written repository code (Baseline Repo) for security and gas comparisons
Ablations: SolAgent w/o Forge, w/o Slither, w/o file-system Tools
Distillation baselines: base Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B, compared to SolAgent-tracker and SolAgent-mix (v1/v2 truncation variants)
